title: Proposal Presentation Framework: Seven Methods for Better Clients and Bigger Projects
summary: Erin Stetzer walked participants through her seven core methods for attracting and serving ideal clients, emphasizing principles like leading with expertise, showing how you think, and delivering consistency. She then presented an actual client proposal from her firm, walking through each section including team introduction, systems, scheduling, budgeting, and references. The session opened into Q&A covering topics such as pre-construction agreements, schedule hold deposits, marketing strategies, and the client-to-project-manager transition.

teachings.explanation: The first priority in any client interaction is to serve their actual need, even if that means directing them to an architect, another resource, or another process before engaging in a building relationship. This is not a sales tactic but a genuine orientation toward improving someone else's situation.
teachings.quote: Whenever a phone call comes in, or whenever I run into someone and have this conversation about working together, it's always about serving first, period.

teachings.explanation: Being someone others want to be around requires deliberate energy and genuine care for people throughout every interaction every day. This magnetism is what draws ideal clients and partners into your orbit naturally.
teachings.quote: We want to be someone that other people like to be around. That requires energy. And that requires caring, right? Caring about other people all day long until you go to sleep from the time you get up to the time you go to sleep.

teachings.explanation: Builders should position themselves as trusted guides rather than salespeople or heroes. Leading with expert knowledge means taking ownership of what you know and using it to improve someone else's situation, acting more like a guiding nurse than a transactional doctor.
teachings.quote: It's not a salesy thing. It's not a sales tactic. It's literally leading with your expert knowledge, your ownership of this knowledge. You're wanting to improve a situation for somebody else.

teachings.explanation: Sharing your perspective, values, and reasoning in everyday conversations—at school drop-off, events, with architects and designers—is a form of marketing and relationship-building. People hire builders partly because of how they think, and unless you share it, no one knows.
teachings.quote: Those are the breadcrumbs right in front of us. We all have breadcrumbs literally in front of us. And those of us that actually pick those breadcrumbs up win.

teachings.explanation: When formally presenting to potential clients, the feeling of organization is what they are seeking. A well-packaged, clearly structured proposal communicates that the project will be controlled and calm, reducing client anxiety about spending a large amount of money.
teachings.quote: The number one thing that I've seen people pick up on is organization. They want to feel how organized you are, right? That is a feeling that they are looking for because they're about to spend a whole bunch of money with you.

teachings.explanation: Working exclusively with high-quality architects, designers, realtors, and vendors elevates your reputation and client experience. Accepting lower-standard partners drags down your own standards, and you should refuse to work with companies that don't meet your bar.
teachings.quote: Work with great companies. Don't work with junkie companies. Don't accept lower standards.

teachings.explanation: Standards are about what you expect of yourself, not what you impose on others. When you hold yourself to a high standard, the right clients and team members who respect those standards are naturally attracted to you and your company.
teachings.quote: Standards are an internal game. And they're, you can judge the standards and you can get better at the standards. And then those who respect and want to be around those standards are attracted to that company or that client.

teachings.explanation: Continuously raising your personal and professional standards—through the relationships you keep, the goals you pursue, and the environments you enter—is essential to becoming the builder and guide that premium clients want to work with. Pay attention to internal nudges toward growth.
teachings.quote: Upgrade our identity, hang out with the people we respect, talk to the people we admire, reach out to those who do better than us, learn.

teachings.explanation: Being the person and partner others can rely on repeatedly—showing up, serving, and following through over and over—is the operating system behind a trusted building business.
teachings.quote: Be the person that shows up, be the partner that serves, be the person others can rely on over and over and over and over again. Deliver consistency consistently.

actionable_advice: After reviewing today's seven methods, take 30 minutes to one hour of quiet time to write out your own personal methodology for how you show up and serve clients.

Structure your formal proposal to include: a table of contents, company and product overview, team introduction with photos, communication and scheduling systems, a weekly report sample, budget management approach, client references, and professional partner references.
Print the budget as a separate PDF from the main proposal book so last-minute pricing updates can be incorporated without reprinting the full presentation.
Print only as many proposal booklets as needed for the specific meeting—avoid leaving extras that can be distributed to competing builders.
Create two versions of your proposal: a shorter handout for initial house walk-throughs and a full bound booklet for the formal budget presentation.

When walking a potential client through a construction site, introduce five or six detail-related points to gauge whether they care about craftsmanship and quality—use this as a filter before handing them your proposal.
Call potential clients a few days after the house walk and directly ask if they are 99% sure they want to work together before investing significant time in full pricing and proposals.

Consider using a non-refundable schedule hold deposit (e.g., $25,000–$50,000) broken down into deliverable-based milestones during long pre-construction phases to protect against clients walking away after months of investment.

Market yourself by visiting showrooms, design centers, tile suppliers, and vendor partners to learn their products—this builds relationships with people who will refer you without it feeling like formal marketing.
Consider hiring a social media team to follow you one day a week and handle posting so you can stay visible online without managing it yourself.
Record internal systems and processes on Loom, then load them into ChatGPT to have those systems refined and documented quickly.
Introduce the project manager to the client after the contract is signed, easing them into a relationship where the project manager becomes the primary point of contact rather than you.
client_challenges: How do you structure the sales process and at what point do clients sign a contract or cut a check before you invest significant time in pricing and proposals?
How do you protect yourself financially when a large project takes six to twelve months of pre-construction work and the client walks away—especially when you are turning down other opportunities?
At what stage of construction should you walk potential clients through a home under construction, and how do you manage the risk of the site not looking its best?
How do you apply this proposal and sales process when you are earlier in your career and don't yet have a large portfolio of high-end work?
When growing the company, what were the most instrumental steps for building relationships with professional references like architects, designers, and vendors?
How do you scale a building company—through more projects, higher project values, or diversifying into adjacent services?
At what point in the client relationship do you introduce and transition clients to your project manager?
Should builders pursue brand sponsorships and partnerships as a marketing channel, and what are the risks of doing so?
notable_quotes: On breadcrumbs and everyday opportunities: 'Those are the breadcrumbs right in front of us. We all have breadcrumbs literally in front of us. And those of us that actually pick those breadcrumbs up win.'
On being a guide rather than the hero: 'The nurses lead the show, right? They're guiding us, guiding us. That doesn't mean be the hero. That means be a guide, be a trusted advocate for someone.'
On standards being internal: 'Standards are an internal game. Those who respect and want to be around those standards are attracted to that company or that client.'
On the shift from who you know to who knows you: 'The world is changing. Like it used to be, you know, it's who you know. Now it's who knows you.'
On brand sponsorships: 'Unless it's something I really believe in and I'm using, I would want to be the one to approach them. That would feel off to me.'
On hustle as the real marketing strategy: 'I do think there's a bottom line—it's pure hustle and it works. Like it's hard, but it works.'
